title: OmniFocus vs Things 3 for Power users
meta_description: OmniFocus vs Things 3 for power users running strict GTD workflows with contexts, review cycles, and automation.
constraint_lens: You need a task manager that supports deep GTD workflows with contexts, perspectives, and automated review structures.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users running strict GTD workflows.
one_second_verdict.reason: Things 3 fails first because contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited.

verdict.summary: OmniFocus wins for power users running strict GTD workflows. It includes contexts, perspectives, and built-in review cycles that support structured task management. Things 3 focuses on clean project lists and areas but limits advanced filtering and automation rules. If contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited, Things 3 fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If contexts, perspectives, and automation rules are structurally limited, Things 3 fails first.

sections.heading: Best fit for strict GTD systems
sections.content: You run advanced GTD workflows with contexts and review cycles. OmniFocus is built around contexts, perspectives, and structured reviews that help maintain a disciplined task system. Things 3 keeps projects and areas simple but removes many of the deeper workflow controls used in strict GTD setups.

bullets.point: Context fields attached to every task
bullets.why_it_matters: You filter tasks by situation or resource such as phone, computer, or errands.

bullets.point: Custom perspective views built from filtering rules
bullets.why_it_matters: You create focused task views for specific workflows without restructuring projects.

bullets.point: Built-in review mode cycling through projects
bullets.why_it_matters: You maintain GTD review habits by checking each project on a scheduled cadence.

bullets.point: Project and area structure with clean visual layout
bullets.why_it_matters: You navigate tasks quickly without configuring complex workflow layers.

bullets.point: Natural language date entry during task creation
bullets.why_it_matters: You add due dates quickly while typing tasks.
You see near-term tasks without building custom filtering views.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You want a lightweight task manager without managing contexts, perspectives, and review workflows.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Things 3 if you prefer a simple project-based task list.

items.fails_when: You need advanced filtering, context grouping, or automated review cycles across many projects.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to OmniFocus for a deeper GTD system.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If your workflow focuses on simple project lists rather than formal GTD systems with contexts and review cycles, Things 3 may feel faster.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: If you run a strict GTD system with contexts and reviews, choose OmniFocus.
If you prefer a clean project list without workflow layers, choose Things 3.
If filtering tasks into custom views is essential, OmniFocus fits better.

faqs.q: Is OmniFocus designed for GTD workflows?
faqs.a: Yes. It includes contexts, perspectives, and review tools built for structured GTD systems.

faqs.q: Does Things 3 support contexts like OmniFocus?
faqs.a: No. Things 3 uses tags and project areas instead of dedicated context fields.

faqs.q: Which tool supports advanced task filtering?
faqs.a: OmniFocus allows custom perspectives that filter tasks using multiple conditions.

faqs.q: When would Things 3 be the better option?
faqs.a: It works well when you want a clean project list without maintaining complex GTD structures.
